title: Riven oak tent peg
subtitle: Green-oak tent peg cleft from a riven billet
excerpt: A tent peg riven from green oak, shaped with a sloyd knife and a side-axe. The wood follows the grain, the peg drives without splitting, and one billet yields a set of six. A first project in riving and a useful piece of kit for any tent or tarp.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on riving and shaping with the froe. Newey and Drage on small green-woodwork projects.

content.text: A tent peg has one job: drive into the ground, hold the guy line against the pull of the tent, come back out without splitting. The keys are
green wood

(so the knife can shape it easily), grain that runs straight along the length (so the peg does not split when struck), and oak (so the point is hard enough to drive into stony ground). The technique is
riving
: splitting the billet along the grain with a froe rather than sawing across it. A riven peg follows the grain perfectly and resists splitting in use.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one; the dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
Source a green oak
billet

: 25 cm long, 10 cm in diameter. A round-cut log from a coppice rotation, a windfall branch, or a section from the tree-surgeon's log pile. The grain must run straight along the length; check by looking at the end-grain for parallel rings and a clear pith centre. A twisted or knotted billet will not rive cleanly. One straight 10 cm billet yields six pegs.

Ash and chestnut are good alternatives with similar working properties. Avoid pine, willow, and birch for tent pegs: pine is soft and the point crushes, willow is too flexible, birch splits in hard ground.
Tools
A
froe
and
beetle
for the riving, a carving axe for rough shaping, a sloyd knife for the finished surfaces, and a strop. No finish is needed: tent pegs live outdoors and the oxidation does the protection.
Workspace

For the riving: a chopping block or a heavy stump, knee-height, in a sheltered space with overhead clearance to swing the beetle. For the knife work: a stable seat with the work on the lap. A shaving horse is helpful but not required for a piece this small.
Method
The
push cut
is the default for the knife work. Strop every 15 minutes.

Stand the billet on end on the chopping block. Place the froe blade across the centre of the end-grain. Strike the back of the froe with the beetle to set the blade 10 mm into the wood. The split starts straight from the pith outwards.

Lever the froe handle side to side to walk the split down the length of the billet. If the split runs out toward one side, lever harder on the side that wants to keep the wood, which pulls the split back toward the centre.

When the billet is in two halves, rive each half into three pegs of roughly 30 mm by 30 mm cross-section. The cross-section can be slightly tapered; the peg only needs to be even at the point and at the head.

Axe the peg roughly. Hold the peg upright on the chopping block. Strike a chamfer off the four corners of one end to start the point. Work down to a four-sided point about 4 cm long, leaving a square shoulder where the point meets the shaft.

Move to the bench and refine the point with push cuts using the sloyd knife. A good tent-peg point is symmetrical four-sided, slightly blunt at the tip (a needle-fine tip splits on the first strike). The shoulder between point and shaft is square and clean.

Cut the head notch. 15 mm down from the head end, on one face only, cut a V-notch 6 mm deep with two push cuts angled in to meet. The notch holds the guy line and prevents it from sliding up off the head when the line tensions.
Round-over the head with thumb-pivot cuts; a slightly domed head spreads the strike of a peg-mallet evenly. Leave the four sides flat down to the notch.
Repeat for the remaining pegs. Six pegs from one billet, a half-hour's work each once you settle into the rhythm.
Finishing

No finish. The pegs are used green and left to season in service: each strike against the ground compresses the surface and stops the wood checking. After the first weekend the pegs are stable and last for years. Stack with the points facing the same way to find them quickly in the bag.
Variations
Longer pegs for sand or shingle: 25 cm peg length, same cross-section. The extra length holds in loose ground that does not bite a short peg.
Shorter pegs for short tarp lines: 12 cm peg length, 20 mm cross-section. A set of four fits in a coat pocket.
Ash or sweet chestnut instead of oak: same method. Ash is lighter to carry; chestnut splits more readily in the riving but resists splitting in use.
Care

Wipe the soil off the points before packing them away. A muddy peg in a closed bag for weeks develops mould on the surface, which does not damage the wood but stains it black. Store them dry in a cotton bag, not plastic.

items.fix: Reset the split: stand the billet on the other end, strike the froe into the partial split from the new direction, and walk it back toward the deflection. Some billets will not rive cleanly; set them aside for firewood and pick a straighter one.
items.cause: Grain twists or a knot deflects the split
items.symptom: Froe split runs out before reaching the end

items.fix: Discard the peg. Check the next billet for parallel rings on the end-grain before splitting.
items.cause: Riven across the grain rather than along it; or grain runs at an angle to the peg length
items.symptom: Peg splits when driven into stony ground

items.fix: Re-rive from a hardwood billet. Pine pegs are fine for one weekend and not much more.
items.cause: Wrong species; pine or birch instead of oak, ash, or chestnut
items.symptom: Point crushes after one weekend in soft ground

items.fix: Recut the notch deeper and lower; the guy line should sit 20 mm below the head when seated in the notch.
items.cause: Notch too shallow or cut too high on the peg
items.symptom: Guy line slips off the head
